result.markdown:
--- title: "Data Types and Classes" subtitle: "Lecture 8" date: "2026-07-10" format: revealjs: output-file: 08-data-types-slides.html html: output-file: 08-data-types.html auto-stretch: false --- ::: {.cell} ::: # Types and classes ## Types and classes An object's *type* indicates how it is stored in memory ## Common data types ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} You'll commonly encounter: - `logical` - `integer` - `double` - `character` ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} You'll less commonly encounter: - `list` - `NULL` - `complex` - `raw` ::: ::: ## Logical and Character {.smaller} ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} **Logical:** Boolean `TRUE` / `FALSE` values - `` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "logical" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "logical" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Character:** single characters, or strings of characters; wrap in quotes - `` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof("x") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof("Hello!") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof("TRUE") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Numeric: Double and integer {.smaller} ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} **Double**: floating point numerical values (default numerical type) - `` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(2.5) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Integer**: integer numerical values; indicated with an `L` - `` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(3L) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "integer" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Type Compatibility {.smaller} Can you use different types of data together? Sometimes... but be careful! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} "3" + 3 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `"3" + 3`: ! non-numeric argument to binary operator ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} 3L + 3 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 6 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(3L + 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} TRUE + 3L ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 4 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(TRUE + 3L) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "integer" ``` ::: ::: ## Concatenation {.smaller} **Vectors** are constructed using the `c` function ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - **Double** vector: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- c(1, 2, 3, 5) typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: ::: - **Integer** vectors: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- c(1L, 2L, 3L, 5L) typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "integer" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} - **Character** vector: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- c("1", "2", "3", "5") typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ::: - **Logical** vectors: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE) typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "logical" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: Specifically, these are called "atomic" vectors in R. Atomic vectors are vectors have elements all of the same type. ## Hierarchy - Does it make sense for the character `"Josh's Sushi"` to be a logical `TRUE/FALSE`? - Does it make sense for the integer `5L` to be a character `"5"`? - Hierarchy: it makes sense to represent a variable higher up as a lower down "rank" - Logical, integer, double, character - Governs converting and coercion ## Converting between types {.smaller} ::: hand without intention... ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} c(2, "Just this one!") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2" "Just this one!" ``` ::: :::
R will happily convert between various types without complaint when different types of data are concatenated in a vector. This is NOT always a good thing. ## Converting between types {.smaller} ::: hand without intention... ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} c(FALSE, 3L) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0 3 ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} c(1.2, 3L) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1.2 3.0 ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} c(2L, "two") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2" "two" ``` ::: ::: ## Converting between types ::: hand with intention... ::: ::: columns ::: {.column width="49%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- 1:3 x ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 2 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "integer" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="49%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y <- as.character(x) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "1" "2" "3" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(y) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Converting between types ::: hand with intention... ::: ::: columns ::: {.column width="49%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} x <- c(TRUE, FALSE) x ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE FALSE ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(x) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "logical" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="49%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y <- as.numeric(x) y ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 0 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(y) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Explicit vs. implicit coercion {.smaller} ::: columns ::: {.column width="49%"} **Explicit coercion:** When you call a function like: - `as.logical()` - `as.numeric()` - `as.integer()` - `as.double()` - `as.character()`. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="49%"} **Implicit coercion:** Happens when you use a vector in a specific context that expects a certain type of vector. ::: ::: ## You've seen explicit coercion before ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="6"} statsci |> pivot_longer( cols = -degree_type, values_to = "n", names_to = "year", names_transform = as.numeric ) ``` ::: ## Back to hierachy ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} as.character(5L) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "5" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} as.character(TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "TRUE" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} as.logical("Josh's Sushi") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] NA ``` ::: ::: # Data classes ## Data classes {.smaller} ::: incremental - Data types are like Lego building blocks - We can stick them together to build more complicated constructs, e.g. *representations of data* - The **class** determines this construct - Examples: factors, dates, and data frames ::: ## Data frames {.smaller} We can think of data frames like like vectors of equal length glued together ::: columns ::: column ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- data.frame(x = 1:2, y = 3:4) df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x y 1 1 3 2 2 4 ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "list" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "data.frame" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: column ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ## alternatively, "bind" two columns together x <- data.frame(x = 1:2) y <- data.frame(y = 3:4) df <- cbind(x, y) df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x y 1 1 3 2 2 4 ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "list" ``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "data.frame" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Data frames {.smaller} We can think of data frames like like vectors of equal length glued together ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- data.frame(x = 1:2, y = 3:4) df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` x y 1 1 3 2 2 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: columns ::: column ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "list" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: column ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "data.frame" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: - When we use the `pull()` function, we extract a vector from the data frame; this is functionally the same as accessing a column with `df$col_name` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> pull(y) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 4 ``` ::: ::: ## Dates ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} today <- as.Date("2026-07-10") today ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "2026-07-10" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "double" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Date" ``` ::: ::: ## More on dates We can think of dates like an integer (the number of days since the origin, 1 Jan 1970) and an integer (the origin, aka "the Unix epoch") glued together ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} as.integer(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20644 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} as.integer(today) / 365 # roughly 56 yrs ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 56.5589 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} as.integer(as.Date("1970-01-01")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0 ``` ::: ::: ## More on dates The `lubridate` package allows you to work with / access elements of dates seamlessly ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(lubridate) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} year(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2026 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} month(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 7 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} day(today) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 10 ``` ::: ::: ## Factors {.scrollable .smaller} - R uses **factors** to handle **categorical variables** with a fixed and known set of possible values - `factor(x = ...)`: *"The default (ordering of levels) is the unique set of values taken by as.character(x), sorted into increasing (alphabetical) order of x"* ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summer_months <- c("June", "July", "June", "August", "June") typeof(summer_months) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} summer_factor <- factor(summer_months) summer_factor ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] June July June August June Levels: August July June ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} levels(summer_factor) ## print levels ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "August" "July" "June" ``` ::: ::: ::: columns ::: {.column .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} typeof(summer_factor) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "integer" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(summer_factor) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "factor" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## More on factors - We can think of factors like character (level labels) and an integer (level numbers) glued together ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} glimpse(summer_factor) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Factor w/ 3 levels "August","July",..: 3 2 3 1 3 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} as.integer(summer_factor) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 2 3 1 3 ``` ::: ::: ## More on factors {.smaller} We can use the **forcats** package (in **tidyverse**) to work with factors! Some commonly used functions are: - fct_relevel(): reorder factors by hand - fct_reorder(): reorder factors by another variable - fct_infreq(): reorder factors by frequency - fct_rev(): reorder factors by reversing ## Example factor re-order ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} amounts <- c("low", "medium", "high", "high", "medium") amounts_factor <- factor(amounts) amounts_factor ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] low medium high high medium Levels: high low medium ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fct_relevel(amounts_factor, c("low", "medium", "high")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] low medium high high medium Levels: low medium high ``` ::: ::: # FAQ ## Quotes VS no quotes VS backticks . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- tibble( x = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `2011` = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `my var` = c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) ) df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 -2 -2 -2 2 -0.5 -0.5 -1 3 0.5 0.5 0 4 1 1 1 5 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: ## Quotes VS no quotes VS backticks {.small .scrollable} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- tibble( x = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `2011` = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `my var` = c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) ) ``` ::: Referencing a column in a pipeline: ::: columns ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter("x" > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 -2 -2 -2 2 -0.5 -0.5 -1 3 0.5 0.5 0 4 1 1 1 5 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `"x"` means the literal character string. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="31%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(x > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 0.5 0.5 0 2 1 1 1 3 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `x` means the column name in `df`. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(`x` > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 0.5 0.5 0 2 1 1 1 3 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `` `x` `` also means the column name in `df`. ::: ::: ## Quotes VS no quotes VS backticks {.small .scrollable} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- tibble( x = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `2011` = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `my var` = c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) ) ``` ::: Referencing a column in a pipeline: ::: columns ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter("2011" > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 -2 -2 -2 2 -0.5 -0.5 -1 3 0.5 0.5 0 4 1 1 1 5 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `"2011"` means the literal character string. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="31%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(2011 > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 -2 -2 -2 2 -0.5 -0.5 -1 3 0.5 0.5 0 4 1 1 1 5 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `2011` means the literal number. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(`2011` > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 0.5 0.5 0 2 1 1 1 3 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `` `2011` `` means the column name in `df`. ::: ::: ## Quotes VS no quotes VS backticks {.small .scrollable} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- tibble( x = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `2011` = c(-2, -0.5, 0.5, 1, 2), `my var` = c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) ) ``` ::: Referencing a column in a pipeline: ::: columns ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter("my var" > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 -2 -2 -2 2 -0.5 -0.5 -1 3 0.5 0.5 0 4 1 1 1 5 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `"my var"` means the literal character string. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="31%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(my var > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in parse(text = input): :2:13: unexpected symbol 1: df |> 2: filter(my var ^ ``` ::: ::: `my var` means nothing. ::: ::: {.column .fragment width="33%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> filter(`my var` > 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 3 x `2011` `my var` 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `` `my var` `` means the column name in `df`. ::: ::: # AE 08: Working with Factors

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
